content: The analysis employs survey-weighted prevalence estimation across nine consecutive NHANES cycles (1999-2000 through 2015-2016) with appropriate complex survey design accounting (sample weights WTMEC2YR, PSU SDMVPSU, strata SDMVSTRA). For multi-cycle trend analysis, 2-year weights are adjusted by dividing by the number of cycles combined. Temporal trends are assessed using survey-weighted logistic regression with NHANES cycle as a continuous predictor to calculate P-for-trend. Serologic classification follows CDC/ACIP standards: vaccine-induced immunity defined as anti-HBs >= 10 mIU/mL with negative anti-HBc (distinguishing vaccine immunity from natural immunity due to past infection). Birth cohort analysis groups participants by estimated birth year (derived from age at exam and survey year) to compare immunity levels across pre- and post-universal infant vaccination cohorts (1994 as pivotal year).
keywords: Survey-weighted prevalence estimation across 9 NHANES cycles
Complex survey design (WTMEC2YR, PSU, strata)
P-for-trend logistic regression for temporal trends
Anti-HBs >= 10 mIU/mL with anti-HBc negative = vaccine-induced immunity
Birth cohort analysis with 1994 universal vaccination pivot

content: Hepatitis B vaccination coverage among US-born children/adolescents (ages 2-18) increased significantly from 62.6% (95% CI: 58.6%-66.4%) in 1999 to 86.3% (95% CI: 82.9%-89.2%) in 2016 (P < .001). However, serologic evidence of vaccine-induced immunity showed paradoxical age-dependent declines: ages 6-10 declined from 64.6% to 46.5%, ages 11-13 from 68.8% to 26.2%, and ages 14-18 from 68.5% to 15.6% over the study period. The youngest group (2-5 years) maintained relatively stable immunity (~60%), consistent with recent vaccination. The 1994-2003 birth cohort demonstrated significantly reduced immunity compared to earlier cohorts. Prevalence of past/present HBV infection remained very low (<1%) across all cycles, confirming the population benefit of universal infant vaccination.
keywords: Vaccination coverage: 62.6% (1999) to 86.3% (2016), P < .001
Ages 14-18 immunity decline: 68.5% to 15.6%
Ages 11-13 immunity decline: 68.8% to 26.2%
Ages 6-10 immunity decline: 64.6% to 46.5%
Ages 2-5 stable immunity (~60%)
1994-2003 birth cohort significantly lower immunity

content: The divergence between increasing vaccination coverage and declining serologic immunity reveals a critical public health challenge: waning vaccine-induced antibody levels with time since vaccination. Older adolescents (14-18 years) who were vaccinated as infants show the most dramatic immunity decline (to 15.6%), as they are furthest from their primary vaccination series. The authors interpret this through the lens of immunological memory: while anti-HBs levels may fall below the 10 mIU/mL protective threshold, anamnestic (booster) responses may still protect against clinical disease. However, this assumption has not been validated in the US birth cohort context. The clinical implication is that booster vaccination may be warranted for young adults born after 1994, particularly those entering high-risk occupations (healthcare) or engaging in high-risk behaviors. The change in vaccine formulations over time (from plasma-derived to recombinant vaccines, and introduction of combination vaccines) may also contribute to differential immunogenicity and antibody durability.
keywords: Waning antibody levels with time since infant vaccination
Immunological memory vs. seroprotection distinction
Booster vaccination consideration for post-1994 birth cohort
Vaccine formulation changes may affect antibody durability
Public health implication for hepatitis B elimination strategy

content: The study addresses several potential confounders and methodological considerations: (1) restriction to US-born participants (DMDBORN=1) eliminates confounding from foreign-born populations with different vaccination histories and natural immunity patterns; (2) serologic classification distinguishes vaccine-induced immunity (anti-HBs+/anti-HBc-) from natural immunity (anti-HBs+/anti-HBc+), preventing misclassification of immunity source; (3) age-stratified analysis separates the biological effect of time since vaccination from period effects; (4) birth cohort analysis further disentangles age, period, and cohort effects on immunity trends. Key limitation acknowledged: anti-HBs levels wane over time but immune memory may persist, so seronegativity does not necessarily equal susceptibility — this creates potential overestimation of the true vulnerability gap.
keywords: US-born restriction eliminates foreign-born confounding
Serologic distinction between vaccine vs. natural immunity
Age-stratified analysis separates time-since-vaccination effect
Birth cohort analysis disentangles age-period-cohort effects
Anti-HBs waning vs. immune memory persistence limitation

content: The study follows STROBE guidelines for serial cross-sectional studies: participant selection criteria are clearly defined (US-born, ages 2-18, complete serology and vaccination data), sample sizes and demographic characteristics are reported for each NHANES cycle, prevalence estimates include 95% confidence intervals throughout, the complex survey design and weighting strategy are specified, and key limitations are acknowledged — including reliance on self/parent-reported vaccination history (potential recall bias), the absence of vaccination records for verification, the cross-sectional design precluding individual-level longitudinal tracking, and the ecological inference limitation of comparing cohort-level trends rather than individual immunity trajectories.
keywords: STROBE compliance for serial cross-sectional design
Sample sizes and demographics reported per cycle
All prevalence estimates with 95% CIs
Self-reported vaccination (recall bias potential)
Limitations: no vaccination records, cross-sectional design
